The factor 24 presumably accounts for 12 encoder + 12 decoder blocks, and the factor 2 accounts for key and value prefixes (standard prefix tuning parameterization). This checks out. However, note that standard prefix tuning (Li & Liang 2021) typically uses a reparameterization via an MLP during training, which increases trainable parameters further, and the 200-token prefix also incurs inference-time cost on attention (O(prefix_len × seq_len)). The 10M figure refers to deployment parameters only. This is consistent with how the paper reports it in Table 1, so not a real issue—just worth noting for completeness. No issue to flag.

progressive__gpt-5.5.label: Progressive (gpt-5.5)
progressive__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: The paper addresses a timely and practically important problem—cost-effective personalization of large code generation models for project-specific unit test generation—and the framing around deployment trade-offs (performance, storage, memory, and training cost) is compelling. The opening is generally clear and accessible, and the comparison among full fine-tuning, lightweight tuning, and prefix tuning is well motivated. However, the contribution is not yet sharply differentiated from existing parameter-efficient fine-tuning work, and the paper should more explicitly state what is novel beyond applying known adaptation methods to a code-generation/test-generation setting. The terminology around “customization” and “personalization” is somewhat loose: the work appears to personalize to projects rather than individual users, and this distinction matters for claims about deployment and privacy. The introduction also makes strong empirical claims (“extensive empirical evaluation,” “significant model improvements”) before providing enough context about datasets, baselines, model scale, statistical rigor, or whether gains reflect true generalization rather than project-level overfitting. The formal definition of customization is mostly tautological and could be replaced with a more precise experimental setup, including train/validation/test splits within projects and cross-project evaluation. Clarity would benefit from tighter writing, fewer repeated explanations of pretraining/fine-tuning, and more careful grammar and terminology. The most significant issue to watch is whether the evaluation convincingly supports the practical deployment claims: the paper must quantify not only accuracy improvements but also storage, latency, training cost, inference overhead, and robustness across diverse projects, while comparing against strong parameter-efficient baselines and clearly explaining when each method is preferable.

comments.title: Lightweight fine-tuning variants are described inconsistently
comments.quote: Specifically, we consider three fine-tuning approaches: (i) _custom_ fine-tuning, which allows all the model parameters to be tuned; (ii) _lightweight_ fine-tuning, which only optimizes the token embedding representations or the final softmax layer; (iii) _prefix_ tuning, which keeps language model parameters frozen, but optimizes a small project-specific vector prefix.
comments.explanation: The summary description of lightweight fine-tuning does not match the later methods. Section 2.3 describes L-EO as tuning the embedding and output layers together, not token embeddings alone or the final softmax layer alone. Section 2.4 describes L-LDB as tuning the last decoder block, including attention, layer normalization, and feedforward layers, not merely the final softmax layer. This could lead readers to misunderstand which parameters are actually trained in the lightweight baselines. The detailed sections mostly resolve the implementation, but the high-level summary should be aligned with them.

comments.title: Formal definition conflates customization data with evaluation data
comments.quote: We use the term _customization_ to refer to the process of fine-tuning a model _m_ , previously trained on a generic dataset for a task _t_ , with the goal of improving its performance on a specific dataset _p_ . The performance of a machine learning model _m_ on a dataset _p_ is measured by one or more evaluation functions _f_ ( _m, p_ ) ... The customization process is designed to modify the trainable parameters of the model _m_ , obtaining the model _m[′]_ , such that the performance of _m[′]_ on _p_ is better than what was attained by _m_ .
comments.explanation: The formal definition uses the same symbol p for the data used to customize the model and the data used to evaluate f(m, p). Later cross-validation suggests the empirical setup is not simply training and testing on the exact same examples, but the notation itself is ambiguous. In standard ML notation, especially for personalization, it is important to distinguish the adaptation/training subset from a held-out project evaluation subset or from the underlying project distribution. Otherwise the stated improvement could be read as improvement on the same dataset used for fine-tuning, which would not demonstrate generalization. A notation such as p_train and p_test, or an explicit project distribution P_p, would remove the ambiguity.

comments.title: Statistical significance is asserted for task-specific metrics without shown support
comments.quote: All customization processes show significant improvement compared to the baseline.
comments.explanation: The paper repeatedly describes task-specific gains as “significant,” including in broad summary statements about both intrinsic and task-specific metrics. The intrinsic metrics are accompanied by statistical tests and p-values, but the task-specific metrics appear to be reported visually and qualitatively, without a corresponding test, confidence interval, or paired analysis. The observed improvements may be substantial and consistent, but in an academic results context “significant” can reasonably be read as statistical significance. The claim should either report appropriate tests for the task-specific metrics or use wording such as “substantial” or “consistent.”

comments.title: Prefix-tuning parameterization is ambiguous relative to the stated 10M count
comments.quote: The technique concatenates a sequence (prefix) of virtual tokens (trainable parameters) to the front of the input of every encoder and decoder block. ... Practically, we set the prefix length to 200 tokens, and thus with an embedding size of 1024, this gives a total of 1024 _×_ 200 _×_ 24 _×_ 2 _≈_ 10M trainable parameters.
comments.explanation: The arithmetic is correct, but the parameterization that justifies the extra factor of 2 is not explained. The prose says the prefix is concatenated to the input of every encoder and decoder block, which would suggest one 200×1024 prefix per block before any additional factors. The ×2 likely corresponds to key/value prefixes in attention, but that is not stated. For an encoder-decoder Transformer, it is also unclear whether prefixes are added to encoder self-attention, decoder self-attention, decoder cross-attention, or all of them. Because the 10M figure is used to compare trainable-parameter budgets, the paper should explicitly state the prefix tensors being optimized and whether any reparameterization network is used.

comments.title: Prefix initialization may use information outside the training fold
comments.quote: The prefix is initialized to the most frequent words in the repository for which the model is customized.
comments.explanation: The paper evaluates models using 4-fold cross-validation within each project, but this sentence does not specify whether the frequent words are computed only from the training portion of each fold, from all non-test source files in the repository, or from the entire repository including held-out test cases/targets. If held-out developer-written tests contribute to the initialization vocabulary, the model could receive evaluation-set vocabulary or style information, which is especially relevant because one downstream metric measures identifier/style similarity. The protocol should specify the data source for frequency counts and ensure it is consistent with the intended train/test separation.

comments.title: Validation procedure within 4-fold cross-validation is underspecified
comments.quote: For each project and fine-tuning strategy ( _e.g.,_ L-EO), we fine-tune and evaluate the models using 4-fold cross-validation. The models are trained until the best validation loss is reached, independently for every fold, every repository, and every customization approach.
comments.explanation: Early stopping on validation loss is standard, but the paper does not explain how the validation set is obtained inside the stated 4-fold cross-validation. If each fold serves as the test fold once and the remaining folds are used for training, early stopping requires a further split of the training folds, a nested validation fold, or some predefined validation data. If the held-out fold is used both for checkpoint selection and reported test performance, the reported metrics would be optimistically biased. The validation split should be specified for reproducibility and to clarify how much per-project data is actually used for customization.

comments.title: Layer-selection rationale from absolute parameter change is only a weak proxy for importance
comments.quote: We observe that, as we go through the transformer model, the average change in parameter values tends to increase, with the last decoder block showing the highest changes in parameter values. As a result, we hypothesize that it could be sufficient to tune the last decoder block and obtain performance improvements similar to the fully custom fine-tuned model.
comments.explanation: The authors present this as a hypothesis and later evaluate L-LDB empirically, so the issue is not fatal. However, average absolute parameter change after full fine-tuning is not necessarily a reliable measure of a layer’s causal importance for adaptation. It can depend on parameter scale, optimizer dynamics, layer normalization, learning-rate effects, and the number or type of parameters in each block. The figure is useful as a heuristic, but the rationale would be stronger with normalization details or ablations over alternative layer subsets.

comments.title: Statistical testing does not match the paired design and pairwise claims
comments.quote: Table 4: Kruskal-Wallis Test p-values testing the significance of the pairwise hypothesis that one customization method is superior than another. Custom strategies are significantly better than baseline.
comments.explanation: The same projects and apparently the same cross-validation folds are evaluated under each baseline/customization strategy, so the observations are naturally paired or repeated-measures rather than independent groups. A standard Kruskal-Wallis test assumes independent groups and is an omnibus test; by itself it does not establish directional pairwise superiority or identify which methods differ without an explicit post-hoc procedure. The table also reports many pairwise comparisons across methods and metrics, but no multiple-comparison correction or statement that p-values are uncorrected is specified. Finally, the paper interprets non-significant differences among customization strategies as being within a “margin of error,” but failure to reject a null hypothesis does not establish practical equivalence without equivalence/non-inferiority analysis or confidence intervals against a predefined margin. A Friedman test, paired Wilcoxon/permutation tests, aligned-rank test, or mixed-effects model with suitable corrections would better match the design.

comments.title: Compute-cost approximation appears to ignore activation-gradient costs through frozen layers
comments.quote: The backward pass takes roughly twice the amount of compute, but it is unnecessary for layers that are frozen.
comments.explanation: Frozen weights avoid computing and storing weight gradients, but that does not necessarily eliminate backward computation through frozen layers. If trainable parameters occur before or inside frozen computation—such as trainable embeddings in L-EO, prefixes inserted into every layer, or other trainable inputs to frozen blocks—one still needs backpropagation through frozen operations to obtain gradients with respect to those trainable parameters. Activation-gradient computation through frozen Transformer layers can be a substantial fraction of training cost. Therefore estimating training FLOPs by omitting the backward pass for all frozen layers may understate the cost of L-EO and prefix tuning and distort the compute comparison.

comments.title: Intrinsic metric computation is underspecified enough to affect reproducibility
comments.quote: We report the BLEU4 score and the mean perplexity per token on the test fold, for all the 20 projects.
comments.explanation: BLEU and perplexity are standard metrics, but their implementation details materially affect reported values. BLEU can vary depending on tokenization, corpus-level versus averaged sentence-level computation, smoothing, case handling, and treatment of generated code formatting. Perplexity depends on whether it is computed over BPE/subword tokens, source-code lexical tokens, or another tokenizer, and whether padding or special tokens are excluded. Because these metrics are central to RQ1 and the statistical comparisons, the paper should specify these choices for reproducibility.

comments.title: Top-5 match metric and decoding procedure are ambiguous
comments.quote: We report the proportion of perfect matches among the top 5 model predictions.
comments.explanation: The intended metric is likely whether at least one of the top-k predictions matches the target for each instance, but the wording could also be read as counting matching predictions among all generated candidates, which would use a different denominator. The method for obtaining the top 5 predictions is also unspecified: beam search versus sampling, beam width, length penalty, duplicate handling, and filtering of invalid outputs can all materially affect perfect-match and abstracted-match rates. Since RQ2 relies on top-k comparisons across models, these decoding and scoring choices should be explicit.

comments.title: Coding-style claims are broader than the identifier-similarity metric supports
comments.quote: For each project’s custom model, we would like to determine how closely the model learns the developer’s personal programming style and preferences. To this end, we extract the collection of all identifiers ... We then pass these text outputs through a tf-idf vectorizer and compute the cosine similarity between them.
comments.explanation: Identifier-based tf-idf cosine similarity is a reasonable proxy for project-specific vocabulary usage, but it does not establish adaptation to developer style in the broader sense. Identifier overlap can reflect project vocabulary, API names, domain terminology, or memorized target tokens rather than formatting, assertion idioms, fixture use, test organization, mocking patterns, or other stylistic features. The paper should narrow the claim to identifier/vocabulary similarity or explicitly frame “coding style” as this limited proxy.

comments.title: Prefix-tuning cost explanation and proposed mitigation are unsupported
comments.quote: Since the prefix parameters suffer from poor initialization, Prefix is the most expensive customization process. ... To overcome this problem, it is possible to first train the prefix on a large generic dataset.
comments.explanation: The results may show that Prefix requires more compute to reach comparable validation loss, but the causal explanation is not established. Earlier, the paper says the prefix is initialized using the most frequent words in the repository, so it is not obvious why “poor initialization” is the demonstrated cause. Other possibilities include the prefix parameterization, prefix length, learning rate, optimization instability, or compute accounting. The proposed remedy—pretraining the prefix on a large generic dataset—is plausible, but no experiment defines the pretraining objective, measures its benefit, or accounts for the additional pretraining cost. These statements should be framed as hypotheses or future work unless supported by ablations.

comments.title: Claim about state-of-the-art code synthesis model scale conflicts with the paper’s own setup
comments.quote: This paper does not evaluate methods for client side training with restricted resources, however, as the most powerful large language models which enable state of the art code synthesis have 10-100 million parameters.
comments.explanation: The stated “10–100 million parameters” scale is inconsistent with the paper’s own experimental model, which has 406M parameters before prefixes. It is also inconsistent with prominent state-of-the-art code-generation models around the relevant period, which were often much larger than 100M parameters. Moreover, 10–100M-parameter models can often be run, at least for inference and sometimes constrained fine-tuning, on consumer hardware, so the resource argument does not follow cleanly from that number. The authors may have intended “10–100 billion” or a broader “hundreds of millions to billions,” but as written the numerical claim is misleading.

progressive_original__gpt-5.5.label: Progressive Original (gpt-5.5)
progressive_original__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: The paper addresses a timely and practically important problem—cost-effective personalization of large code generation models for project-specific unit test generation—and the framing around deployment trade-offs (performance, storage, memory, and training cost) is compelling. The opening is generally clear and accessible, and the comparison among full fine-tuning, lightweight tuning, and prefix tuning is well motivated. However, the contribution is not yet sharply differentiated from existing parameter-efficient fine-tuning work, and the paper should more explicitly state what is novel beyond applying known adaptation methods to a code-generation/test-generation setting. The terminology around “customization” and “personalization” is somewhat loose: the work appears to personalize to projects rather than individual users, and this distinction matters for claims about deployment and privacy. The introduction also makes strong empirical claims (“extensive empirical evaluation,” “significant model improvements”) before providing enough context about datasets, baselines, model scale, statistical rigor, or whether gains reflect true generalization rather than project-level overfitting. The formal definition of customization is mostly tautological and could be replaced with a more precise experimental setup, including train/validation/test splits within projects and cross-project evaluation. Clarity would benefit from tighter writing, fewer repeated explanations of pretraining/fine-tuning, and more careful grammar and terminology. The most significant issue to watch is whether the evaluation convincingly supports the practical deployment claims: the paper must quantify not only accuracy improvements but also storage, latency, training cost, inference overhead, and robustness across diverse projects, while comparing against strong parameter-efficient baselines and clearly explaining when each method is preferable.

comments.title: Lightweight fine-tuning variants are described inconsistently
comments.quote: Specifically, we consider three fine-tuning approaches: (i) _custom_ fine-tuning, which allows all the model parameters to be tuned; (ii) _lightweight_ fine-tuning, which only optimizes the token embedding representations or the final softmax layer; (iii) _prefix_ tuning, which keeps language model parameters frozen, but optimizes a small project-specific vector prefix.
comments.explanation: At first I read this as a high-level grouping: there are three families of approaches, with lightweight fine-tuning containing multiple subvariants. That framing is reasonable, and later sections do indeed introduce two lightweight methods. However, the specific description here does not match those later methods. Section 2.3 describes L-EO as tuning the embedding and output layers together, not token embeddings alone or the final softmax layer alone. Section 2.4 describes L-LDB as tuning the last decoder block, including self-attention, encoder-decoder attention, layernorm, and feedforward layers, not merely the final softmax layer. The abstract has a related phrase, “the token embeddings and softmax layer or the final layer alone,” which could perhaps mean “last decoder block,” but the introduction’s “final softmax layer” makes that interpretation difficult. This is not just a wording preference: it could lead a reader to misunderstand which parameters are actually trained in the lightweight baselines. The later detailed sections mostly resolve the implementation, but the summary statement should be aligned with them.

comments.title: Formal definition conflates the project dataset used for customization with the evaluation dataset
comments.quote: We use the term _customization_ to refer to the process of fine-tuning a model _m_ , previously trained on a generic dataset for a task _t_ , with the goal of improving its performance on a specific dataset _p_ . The performance of a machine learning model _m_ on a dataset _p_ is measured by one or more evaluation functions _f_ ( _m, p_ ), where _f_ can be either a maximization ( _e.g.,_ BLEU, top-k accuracy) or minimization ( _e.g.,_ perplexity) function. The customization process is designed to modify the trainable parameters of the model _m_ , obtaining the model _m[′]_ , such that the performance of _m[′]_ on _p_ is better than what was attained by _m_ .
comments.explanation: The authors’ intent seems to be to define customization at the project level: a project p supplies data for adaptation, and the customized model should perform better on that project’s distribution. Later experimental context helps, because the models are evaluated with 4-fold cross-validation, so the empirical comparison is not simply train-and-test on the exact same examples. Still, the formal definition uses the same symbol p for the data being fine-tuned on and the data used in f(m, p). In standard ML notation, especially for personalization, it is important to distinguish the adaptation/training subset from the held-out project evaluation subset or from the underlying project distribution. As written, the inequality can be read as requiring improvement on the same dataset used for fine-tuning, which would not by itself demonstrate generalization and could be satisfied by overfitting. A small notation refinement, e.g. p_train and p_test or an explicit project distribution P_p, would remove the ambiguity.

comments.title: “Significant” improvements on task-specific metrics may overstate the shown statistical support
comments.quote: In our extensive empirical evaluation we found that all the customization strategies lead to significant model improvements on a target project in terms of both intrinsic and task-specific metrics.
comments.explanation: The broad empirical message is supported by the later results: the customized models improve BLEU/perplexity substantially and also improve perfect matches, abstracted matches, and identifier-style similarity. I also checked whether the word “significant” is later backed by statistical testing. For intrinsic metrics, the paper reports Kruskal-Wallis tests and p-values against the baseline. For task-specific metrics, the later context describes large improvements visually and qualitatively, but does not show an analogous statistical test in the provided sections. If “significant” is meant colloquially as “large,” the sentence is acceptable; however, in an academic results summary, readers may reasonably interpret it as statistical significance for both metric classes. The claim would be safer if phrased as “substantial improvements” for task-specific metrics, or if corresponding statistical tests were reported.

comments.title: Prefix-tuning parameterization is ambiguous relative to the stated 10M count
comments.quote: The technique concatenates a sequence (prefix) of virtual tokens (trainable parameters) to the front of the input of every encoder and decoder block. In our context, the intuition behind this approach is that the prefix embeds the properties of a specific project, which allows the model to generate customized responses for that repository. Practically, we set the prefix length to 200 tokens, and thus with an embedding size of 1024, this gives a total of 1024 _×_ 200 _×_ 24 _×_ 2 _≈_ 10M trainable parameters.
comments.explanation: The arithmetic in the displayed count is correct: 1024×200×24×2 is about 9.8M. What remains unclear is the exact prefix-tuning parameterization that makes this the right count. The prose says the prefix is concatenated to the input of every encoder and decoder block, which would suggest one 200×1024 prefix per block, i.e. roughly 1024×200×24 parameters before any extra factors. The additional ×2 presumably corresponds to key/value prefixes in attention, as in common implementations of Li and Liang-style prefix tuning, but that is not stated. For an encoder-decoder Transformer there is also a possible distinction among encoder self-attention, decoder self-attention, and decoder cross-attention; if prefixes are added to all relevant attention modules, the count could differ. This matters because Table 1 uses the 10M figure to compare methods by trainable-parameter budget. The issue is likely fixable by explicitly stating whether prefixes are learned as layer-wise key/value tensors, whether decoder cross-attention receives its own prefix, and whether any reparameterization MLP is used.

comments.title: Prefix initialization may use information outside the training fold
comments.quote: The prefix is initialized to the most frequent words in the repository for which the model is customized.
comments.explanation: The idea of initializing project-specific prefixes from frequent project vocabulary is plausible and well motivated for personalization. However, in the experimental setup the models are evaluated with 4-fold cross-validation within each project. This sentence does not specify whether the frequent words are computed only from the training portion of the fold, from all non-test source files in the repository, or from the entire repository including held-out test cases/targets. If the initialization uses identifiers or tokens from developer-written tests in the held-out fold, it could leak evaluation-set vocabulary and style into the model, especially because one of the reported downstream metrics is identifier/style similarity. If the intended deployment scenario assumes access to the whole repository but not the specific target tests, that should be stated and the data source for frequency counts should be restricted accordingly. As written, the protocol is ambiguous in a way that could affect the validity of the reported personalization gains.

comments.title: Validation procedure within 4-fold cross-validation is underspecified
comments.quote: For each project and fine-tuning strategy ( _e.g.,_ L-EO), we fine-tune and evaluate the models using 4-fold cross-validation. The models are trained until the best validation loss is reached, independently for every fold, every repository, and every customization approach.
comments.explanation: The authors correctly separate evaluation by project and by customization method, and early stopping on validation loss is a standard choice. The unresolved point is how the validation set is obtained inside the stated 4-fold cross-validation. In ordinary 4-fold CV, each fold serves as the test fold once and the remaining folds are used for training; early stopping then requires either a further split of the training folds, a nested validation fold, or some predefined validation data. If the held-out fold is used both for selecting the best checkpoint and reporting test performance, the reported metrics would be optimistically biased. If an internal split is used, the paper should specify it because it affects the amount of per-project customization data, comparability across methods, and reproducibility.

comments.title: Layer-selection rationale from absolute parameter change is only a weak proxy for importance
comments.quote: We observe that, as we go through the transformer model, the average change in parameter values tends to increase, with the last decoder block showing the highest changes in parameter values. As a result, we hypothesize that it could be sufficient to tune the last decoder block and obtain performance improvements similar to the fully custom fine-tuned model.
comments.explanation: The authors are careful to phrase this as a hypothesis, and they later evaluate L-LDB empirically, so this is not a fatal methodological flaw. Still, the motivation is somewhat underjustified. Average absolute parameter change after full fine-tuning is not necessarily a reliable measure of a layer’s causal importance for adaptation: it can depend on parameter scale, optimizer dynamics, layer normalization, learning-rate effects, and the number/type of parameters in each block. A layer that changes less in a full fine-tune may still be essential, and a layer that changes more may simply be more sensitive under the optimizer. The figure is useful as a heuristic, but the text risks making the last-decoder-block choice seem more directly supported than the evidence warrants unless the authors add normalization details or present an ablation over alternative layer subsets.

comments.title: Kruskal-Wallis is not sufficient for the stated pairwise and repeated-measures comparisons
comments.quote: Next, we perform statistical tests to investigate whether the observed differences between the baseline and custom models are significant, as well as differences among the customization approaches. Specifically, we rely on the Kruskal-Wallis test, a non-parametric statistical test.
comments.explanation: I understand the authors' intent: they want a non-parametric test because BLEU/perplexity distributions over projects or folds may not be normal. However, the experimental design evaluates the same projects, and apparently the same cross-validation folds, under each customization strategy and the baseline. That makes the observations paired/repeated-measures rather than independent samples, whereas the standard Kruskal-Wallis test assumes independent groups. A Friedman test, aligned-rank test, paired Wilcoxon/permutation tests, or a mixed-effects model would better respect the paired structure. In addition, Kruskal-Wallis is an omnibus test; by itself it does not establish which specific method differs from baseline or which customization strategies differ from each other without post-hoc pairwise tests and multiple-comparison correction. This matters because the paper later interprets the tests as confirming baseline-vs-method improvements and no differences among methods.

comments.title: Compute-cost approximation appears to ignore activation-gradient costs through frozen layers
comments.quote: The backward pass takes roughly twice the amount of compute, but it is unnecessary for layers that are frozen.
comments.explanation: The high-level idea of separating forward and backward costs for trainable versus frozen parameters is reasonable, and frozen weights do avoid computing and storing weight gradients. But the statement is too strong for several of the methods studied. If trainable parameters occur before or inside frozen computation—e.g., trainable embeddings in L-EO, prefixes inserted into every layer, or any trainable input to a frozen block—one still needs backpropagation through frozen operations to obtain gradients with respect to those trainable inputs or prefix vectors. Even when weight gradients are omitted, activation-gradient computation through frozen transformer layers can be a substantial fraction of the backward cost. Thus estimating training FLOPs by omitting the backward pass for all frozen layers may substantially understate the cost of L-EO and prefix tuning, and potentially distort the RQ3 comparison.

comments.title: Intrinsic metric computation is underspecified enough to affect reproducibility
comments.quote: We report the BLEU4 score and the mean perplexity per token on the test fold, for all the 20 projects.
comments.explanation: The choice of BLEU4 and perplexity is standard, so the intent is clear. What remains underspecified is how these metrics are computed. BLEU can vary substantially depending on tokenization, whether it is corpus-level BLEU or averaged sentence-level BLEU, smoothing, case handling, and treatment of generated code formatting. Perplexity likewise depends on whether it is computed over BPE/subword tokens, source-code lexical tokens, or another tokenizer, and whether padding/special tokens are excluded. Because the reported BLEU and perplexity values are central to RQ1 and later statistical comparisons, these implementation choices are not merely cosmetic; a reader would need them to reproduce or interpret the results.

comments.title: Top-5 match metric is ambiguous and decoding details are missing
comments.quote: We report the proportion of perfect matches among the top 5 model predictions.
comments.explanation: The surrounding text and later reference to top-k accuracy suggest that the intended metric is likely whether at least one of the top-k predictions matches the target for each instance. However, the wording 'proportion of perfect matches among the top 5 model predictions' could also be read as counting matching predictions among all generated candidates, which would be a different denominator and interpretation. In addition, the method for obtaining the top 5 predictions is not specified here: beam search versus sampling, beam width, length penalty, duplicate handling, and whether invalid outputs are filtered can all materially affect perfect-match and abstracted-match rates. Since RQ2 relies on top-k comparisons across models, these choices should be made explicit.

comments.title: Task-specific improvements are called significant without a described statistical test
comments.quote: All customization processes show significant improvement compared to the baseline.
comments.explanation: The plotted improvements described in the paragraph sound large and plausible, and the authors also state that improvements occur for every project. Still, in this context the word 'significant' is potentially misleading because the preceding RQ1 section explicitly discusses statistical significance, whereas no statistical test, confidence interval, or paired analysis for the RQ2 metrics is described in the passage. If 'significant' is intended in the statistical sense, the paper should specify the test and correction procedure. If it is intended informally, wording such as 'substantial' or 'consistent' would avoid overclaiming.

comments.title: Non-significance is overinterpreted as being within a margin of error
comments.quote: However, we do not observe statistical significance in the differences among the customization strategies, meaning that, in terms of intrinsic metrics performances, the differences are within margin of error.
comments.explanation: It is reasonable to report that the authors did not find statistically significant differences among customization strategies. The concern is the interpretation. A failure to reject a null hypothesis does not by itself show that differences are within a meaningful margin of error or practically equivalent; that would require an equivalence/non-inferiority analysis or at least confidence intervals around the pairwise differences and a pre-specified practical margin. This is especially relevant because Table 3 shows nontrivial average BLEU differences between some strategies, even if they may not be statistically reliable under the chosen test.

comments.title: Coding-style metric supports lexical similarity, not necessarily developer style
comments.quote: For each project’s custom model, we would like to determine how closely the model learns the developer’s personal programming style and preferences. To this end, we extract the collection of all identifiers ( _i.e.,_ variables and functions’ names) from the unit tests written by the developer as well as those generated by the models. We then pass these text outputs through a tf-idf vectorizer and compute the cosine similarity between them.
comments.explanation: The proposed identifier-based tf-idf similarity is a reasonable lightweight proxy for one aspect of project-specific adaptation, and the authors correctly describe it later as vocabulary similarity. The concern is that the earlier framing as 'developer’s personal programming style and preferences' is broader than what the metric can establish. Identifier overlap can reflect copied or memorized project vocabulary, API names, exact/near-exact target overlap, or domain terminology, rather than style in the usual programming-language sense, which might include formatting, assertion patterns, test structure, fixture use, and idioms. The method is useful, but the claim should be narrowed to identifier/vocabulary similarity unless additional style features are analyzed.

comments.title: Kruskal-Wallis test is not well matched to the paired experimental design or directional pairwise claims
comments.quote: Table 4: Kruskal-Wallis Test p-values testing the significance of the pairwise hypothesis that one customization method is superior than another. Custom strategies are significantly better than baseline.
comments.explanation: The table is trying to support an important conclusion: every customization method improves over the baseline, while the customization methods are not significantly different from one another. That is a reasonable thing to test, and the direction of the observed BLEU/perplexity differences in Table 3 is consistent with the prose. However, the stated test raises two methodological concerns that are not resolved by the surrounding context. First, Kruskal-Wallis is conventionally an omnibus non-parametric test for differences among independent groups; it does not by itself test a directional hypothesis that one method is “superior” to another. Pairwise comparisons would normally require an explicit post-hoc procedure or a pairwise rank test, and the direction of superiority should be established separately from the p-value. Second, the measurements are naturally paired/repeated across the same projects and folds: baseline, Custom, L-EO, L-LDB, and Prefix are evaluated on the same project/fold structure. A paired non-parametric test such as Friedman for all methods, followed by Wilcoxon signed-rank/Nemenyi-style post-hoc comparisons, would more directly respect the design. Treating the samples as independent can misestimate significance. Because this table underpins the central claim that all customized models significantly outperform the baseline, the issue is methodologically important even if the very small p-values may make the baseline-vs-custom conclusion robust in practice.

comments.title: No multiple-comparison procedure is specified for the pairwise p-values
comments.quote: Kruskal-Wallis Test p-values testing the significance of the pairwise hypothesis that one customization method is superior than another.
comments.explanation: I first read Table 4 as a compact matrix of pairwise comparisons for BLEU and perplexity, which is useful for seeing whether the customized methods differ from each other. But because the table reports many pairwise tests across five methods and two metrics, a reader would expect some mention of family-wise error or false-discovery-rate control, or a clear statement that these are uncorrected exploratory p-values. The context says the authors rely on Kruskal-Wallis tests, but it does not indicate any post-hoc correction. For the baseline comparisons, the reported p-values are so small that correction would likely not change the qualitative result. For comparisons among customization strategies, however, the interpretation of non-significance and any near-threshold results should still be tied to an explicit procedure. This is localized and fixable, but the current presentation leaves the statistical inference underspecified.

comments.title: Task-specific “significant improvement” is asserted without a described statistical test
comments.quote: All customization processes show significant improvement compared to the baseline. Specifically, these improvements are observed for every single project (full results will be available on our online appendix).
comments.explanation: The qualitative pattern in Figure 3 appears consistent with the authors’ claim: the customized models have higher exact and abstracted match rates than the baseline, and the effect seems practically meaningful. I also checked whether the earlier methods section specified statistical testing for RQ2, but it only defines the task-specific metrics; the explicit statistical testing discussion is attached to RQ1 and the intrinsic metrics. Thus, the word “significant” is ambiguous here. If the authors mean statistically significant, the paper needs to state the test, unit of analysis, pairing, and correction strategy. If they mean merely substantial or consistent, the wording should avoid the statistical implication. The statement that improvements occur for every project is helpful, but it is not the same as a statistical significance test, especially for top-k match proportions that may have different variances across projects and folds.

comments.title: Prefix tuning’s higher cost is attributed to “poor initialization” without supporting evidence
comments.quote: Since the prefix parameters suffer from poor initialization, Prefix is the most expensive customization process.
comments.explanation: The surrounding figure and text do support the descriptive observation that Prefix requires more compute to reach comparable validation loss in these experiments. What remains under-justified is the causal explanation. Earlier, the paper says the prefix is initialized using the most frequent words in the repository, which sounds like a deliberate heuristic rather than an obviously poor initialization. The passage does not report an ablation comparing random initialization, frequent-token initialization, pre-trained prefixes, or alternative prefix lengths/learning rates. Without such evidence, it is not clear whether Prefix is expensive because of initialization specifically, because prefix tuning optimizes a harder parameterization, because the hyperparameters are less tuned, because 200 virtual tokens introduce optimization instability, or because the compute accounting treats prefix operations differently. The claim could be made safer by saying the observed curves are consistent with an initialization/optimization difficulty, rather than presenting poor initialization as established.

comments.title: Speculative mitigation for prefix cost is presented without experimental support
comments.quote: To overcome this problem, it is possible to first train the prefix on a large generic dataset. Then, given proper hyperparameter tuning, it is possible to substantially cut down compute cost for customizing the prefix.
comments.explanation: The suggestion is plausible: prior work on prompt/prefix tuning often benefits from better initialization or multi-task pretraining. However, in this paper’s experimental context it appears to be speculative. The authors do not report experiments with generic prefix pretraining, do not define the proposed pretraining objective or dataset, and do not quantify how much compute would be saved after including the cost of pretraining. The phrase “substantially cut down” therefore overstates what has been demonstrated here. This does not undermine the main experimental comparison, but it does weaken the deployment-cost discussion for Prefix unless framed explicitly as a hypothesis or future optimization.

comments.title: Claim that state-of-the-art code synthesis models have 10–100 million parameters conflicts with the paper’s own model scale
comments.quote: This paper does not evaluate methods for client side training with restricted resources, however, as the most powerful large language models which enable state of the art code synthesis have 10-100 million parameters.
comments.explanation: I considered whether this might be an OCR error, but the surrounding sentence makes the number consequential: it is used to argue that such models cannot reasonably run on consumer laptops. As written, “10-100 million parameters” is inconsistent with the paper’s own experimental setup, which uses a 406M-parameter BART model before prefixes. It is also inconsistent with the broader state of large language models for code around the ICLR 2022 period, where prominent systems such as Codex/GPT-style models were far larger than 100M parameters. Moreover, 10–100M-parameter models can often be run, at least for inference and sometimes fine-tuning with constraints, on consumer hardware, so the resource argument does not follow cleanly from that scale. The authors may have intended “10–100 billion” or a broader “hundreds of millions to billions,” but as written the numerical claim is wrong and weakens the justification for excluding client-side training.

comments.title: Statistical significance is asserted for task-specific metrics without shown support
comments.quote: In our extensive empirical evaluation we found that all the customization strategies lead to significant model’s improvements on a target project, in terms of both intrinsic and task-specific metrics, with the custom models adapting to the coding style of the target project.
comments.explanation: I initially read "significant" here in the ordinary academic sense of statistically significant. Looking back at the reported methodology and results, the paper does support statistical significance for intrinsic metrics: Section 3.2 says Kruskal-Wallis tests are used, and Section 4.1 reports p-values below 1e-7 for BLEU/perplexity against the baseline. For the task-specific metrics in Section 4.2, however, the provided context reports aggregate improvements in perfect matches, abstracted matches, and tf-idf cosine similarity, but does not describe a statistical test or confidence interval establishing significance for those metrics. The word could be intended informally as 'large' or 'meaningful,' and the empirical direction of the improvements is consistent with the claim. Still, in a conclusion, this phrasing can reasonably be read as extending the statistical-significance claim from RQ1 to RQ2 without the same support. A safer formulation would distinguish statistically significant intrinsic improvements from observed/substantial task-specific improvements, unless the omitted parts of the paper contain corresponding tests.

comments.title: Coding-style adaptation claim is broader than the measured evidence
comments.quote: with the custom models adapting to the coding style of the target project.
comments.explanation: The paper does measure a style-related quantity, and the conclusion is clearly trying to summarize that result. However, checking the earlier definition, the 'Coding Style' metric is specifically cosine similarity between tf-idf vectors built from identifiers in developer-written tests and generated tests. That is evidence that customized models use more similar identifier vocabulary, not necessarily that they adapt to broader coding style dimensions such as formatting conventions, assertion idioms, test organization, mocking patterns, naming schemas beyond token overlap, or API usage style. Because 'coding style' is a broad term in software engineering, a careful reader could take this as stronger than what was evaluated. This is fixable by saying the models adapt to the target project's identifier usage or to the paper's coding-style proxy.
